source.title: Dissociating pain from its anticipation in the human brain

confidence_rationale: Canonical early paper establishing that pain anticipation activates distinct-but-adjacent sites to pain experience itself (medial frontal lobe, insular cortex, cerebellum). Foundational for all subsequent predictive-coding-in-pain work. Old (1999) but repeatedly replicated, and cited as the origin point by essentially all papers in this corpus.
failure_signatures: A single unified pain forecast-plus-experience substrate is falsified by the anatomical separation of anticipation and experience sites.
Early paper with modest N and first-generation fMRI; replication in larger modern studies is what gives it durable weight.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Anticipation of pain activates sites within medial frontal lobe, insular cortex, and cerebellum that are distinct from, but adjacent to, the sites mediating pain experience itself.
mapping.ree_translation: Biological precedent for keeping the forward predictor (E2_harm_a / E2_harm_s) functionally distinct from the consumer (dACC-analog SD-032b). Supports that the prediction machinery and the experience machinery are not the same module. Less direct evidence on whether there are one or two forward predictors.
mapping.mapping_caveat: 1999 study with first-generation fMRI and small N by modern standards. Adjacent-but-distinct anatomy in Science-era fMRI is a coarse spatial claim.
mapping.source_context: Human fMRI, cued pain anticipation, healthy adults.

confidence_components.notes: Downweighted for being an older small-N fMRI study, upweighted because it is canonical and has been replicated at larger scales since.
